title: Simple Red Moong Dal

overview.description: A simple, comforting dal made from split red moong dal and finished with a fragrant tempering of ghee, cumin, garlic and dried chilli.

method: Rinse the moong dal several times until the water runs relatively clear.
Place the dal, water and turmeric in a saucepan.
Bring to the boil, then reduce the heat.
Simmer gently until the dal is completely soft and beginning to break down.
Stir occasionally and add a little extra water if necessary.
Add the chopped tomato and onion if using.
Continue cooking until the vegetables are soft and the dal has reached a thick, spoonable consistency.
Whisk or stir the dal to create the desired texture.
For the tadka, heat the ghee in a small frying pan.
When they begin to sizzle, add the sliced garlic and dried chilli.
Cook until the garlic is lightly golden.
Add the asafoetida if using and cook for a few seconds.
Pour the hot tadka over the dal.
Finish with fresh coriander and lemon juice if using.

learning.did-you-know: Moong dal cooks more quickly than many other pulses and naturally breaks down into a smooth, creamy texture without needing dairy.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to cook a simple pulse until tender and how a tadka can transform a mild base with a small amount of intensely flavoured ingredients.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A simple foundation recipe that introduces the children to dal and the idea that a small finishing tempering can completely change the flavour of a dish.
chapman-tips: Rinsing the dal removes dust and excess starch and helps produce a cleaner flavour.
Moong dal can go from firm to completely soft quite quickly, so start checking it early.
The dal will thicken considerably as it cools; make it slightly thinner than the final consistency you want.
The tadka should be poured over the dal while both are hot.
Don't burn the garlic. Light golden is sweet and fragrant; dark brown can become bitter.
The dried chilli can be left whole for flavour with very little heat, or broken up for more heat.
This is a good recipe for children to learn how texture changes as a pulse cooks.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Cool completely before freezing in airtight portions. Thaw overnight in the refrigerator and reheat gently, adding a little water if necessary.
notes.leftovers: Thin with water and reheat as a soup, serve with rice or use as a filling for a savoury flatbread.
